headline: MGX closes $49B AI fund - one of the largest ever
detail: Massive institutional capital flowing into AI infrastructure keeps the secular bid under chip and cloud names. This directly supports the SMH, SOXL, and CRWD thesis. Large fund closes often precede deployment into public equities, which can sustain momentum in AI-adjacent names.
rec: Nothing to force today, but this supports holding SMH and SOXL at current levels. If SMH pulls back to the $630s, that's a higher-conviction add opportunity - scanner doesn't show SMH but AMAT (entry $721.55-722.28, stop $720.47) is a Grade A daily-up chip play that aligns with this theme if it triggers.

headline: Anthropic gets export control relief ahead of IPO
detail: Trump administration lifting controls on Claude models means US AI companies can sell globally without restriction - expands their addressable market overnight. This is broadly bullish for AI infrastructure demand, which means more chips, more servers, more spend flowing to names like NVDA and AMD. Positive read-through for your SMH and SOXL positions.
rec: No immediate trade needed - this is a tailwind, not a trigger. Watch if NVDA responds with a move through $200; scanner entry $198.39-198.62 with stop $197.35 is the level to watch for a small add if you want exposure.

headline: Microsoft planning thousands of layoffs while spending on AI
detail: Msft cutting headcount while ramping AI capex is the clearest signal yet that big tech is reallocating dollars from labor to chips and compute. That's direct demand for NVDA, AMAT, and the broader semiconductor supply chain. Short-term the layoff headline can spook broader sentiment, but the capex story is what matters for your book.
rec: No action needed now - this reinforces the chip demand story underpinning SMH and SOXL. Monitor whether the layoff news creates a dip in QQQ; your stop at $670 gives plenty of room but a flush toward $700 could be a add point, not a panic point.

headline: Tech led H1 gains but biggest winners weren't US
detail: International tech outperforming US in H1 is a mild warning that the easy domestic tech trade may be maturing or that rotation risk is building. Doesn't change your names today but is a reason not to add more concentrated tech exposure at current premium levels. Your book is already ~90% tech on margin - this is a diversification flag, not a crisis.
rec: No trade, but worth noting: resist the urge to add new tech names just because the scanner shows setups. You're already concentrated and on margin - the risk is asymmetric on the downside.
